nativeRange.habitat: Shallow Caribbean reef slopes, reef flats, and fore-reef environments with moderate current; typically anchored to hard substrate in areas with consistent water movement

decorPreferences: secured with underwater epoxy or gel glue
adequate spacing from neighboring corals and anemones

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae (zooxanthellate), supplemented by filter feeding. Has been known to benefit from weekly target feedings of zooplankton such as baby brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, Marine Snow, PhytoPlan, and other suitable micro-foods for filter-feeding invertebrates. Calcium, iodine, strontium, and trace element supplementation is generally recommended to support skeletal growth and polyp health.

avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals in direct contact
other gorgonian species in direct contact
corals prone to chemical warfare without spacing

imageCandidates.notes: James St. John. 4000x3000. Vibrant purple/magenta feathery sea plume colony (Pseudopterogorgia/Antillogorgia sp.) in shallow water over white sand; sharp, whole colony, textbook 'flowing feathery purple branches' look - best representative of the genus.
Robin White. 2048x2022. Antillogorgia bipinnata (Bipinnate Sea Plume), night in-situ; sharp, whole colony showing the bipinnate feather-branch structure against black water. Small corner watermark.
Haplochromis. 1602x2580. Antillogorgia acerosa sea plumes in an aquarium display with a blue chromis; whole plumes, golden-tan coloration, shows how it is kept.
Luke Padon. 1369x1170. Antillogorgia rigida, whole plume colony over reef in shallow water with clear lavender/purple coloration - adds a distinct species and the purple hue.

Diego Delso. 5587x6487. Antillogorgia acerosa branch close-up, in-water; very high resolution and tack-sharp, clearly showing the pink/purple pinnate feather structure and polyp detail. Tight framing so kept as non-recommended detail shot.

Thibaud Aronson. 1024x768. Antillogorgia bipinnata on a natural Caribbean reef, whole colony in-situ showing the feather-plume growth form; slightly muted (deep-water) color so kept as non-recommended natural-habitat view.

summary: The Purple Whip Gorgonian is a photosynthetic Caribbean soft coral prized for its flowing, feathery purple branches. It requires strong, non-laminar water movement, moderate lighting, and occasional supplemental feeding, and is generally considered intermediate-level to keep.
careNotes:
Hobbyists browsing livestock listings will encounter this coral under two scientific names: the older Pseudopterogorgia, which remains widespread in trade and in much of the aquarium literature, and the more current Antillogorgia, the genus into which all 11 photosynthetic, feather-branched Caribbean sea plume species were transferred following a 2012 taxonomic revision. Species commonly sold include Antillogorgia bipinnata (Bipinnate Sea Plume) and A. elisabethae (Deichmann's Sea Plume). The name Pseudopterogorgia now applies to non-photosynthetic Indo-Pacific relatives, so both names remain in circulation and refer to different animals.

Key care requirements:
• Lighting: Moderate intensity is generally advised — approximately 100–200 PAR. Placement in the middle portion of the aquarium is typical. New specimens tend to benefit from gradual light acclimation rather than immediate high-intensity placement.
• Flow: High, non-laminar (chaotic or alternating) water movement is considered critical. Strong flow prevents cyanobacteria and algae buildup on the branches, which can interfere with polyp extension and feeding. Laminar flow or dead spots have been reported to cause cyano fouling and rapid decline.
• Cyanobacteria risk: Cyanobacteria (a slimy, reddish-purple film that can coat the branches) is one of the most common failure modes in captivity. Branches should be inspected regularly; if the film appears, the colony should be gently rinsed and water movement around the specimen increased.
• Anchoring: The base should be secured to live rock using aquarium-safe underwater epoxy or gel glue so the colony can orient into the current. A freestanding upright position allows polyps maximum exposure to both light and flow.
• Trace elements: Calcium, iodine, strontium, and other trace elements are generally recommended to support the protein core and axial structure.
• Tank size: Wild colonies of Antillogorgia bipinnata can reach 57 cm or more, and A. elisabethae up to 2 m. In aquaria, colonies typically grow 15–60 cm.
breedingNotes: Fragmentation (fragging) has been documented in captive aquaculture settings. Some specialty aquaculture operations collect small wild fragments from Caribbean reefs and grow them under controlled conditions before retail sale. Sexual reproduction occurs on natural reefs, but deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

additional.name: OceansGarden Aquaculture – Eco-Friendly Purple Plume Gorgonian (Pseudopterogorgia bipinnata)
additional.notes: Confirmed care level (Moderate), temperament (Peaceful), reef-safe status, diet (filter feeder), placement (Middle), and minimum tank size (10 gal cited, though adopted 150L as conservative larger value given colony growth potential). Secondary source cross-check on salinity 1.024–1.026 and general parameters.

additional.name: TankFacts – Purple Whip Gorgonian (Pseudopterogorgia sp.)
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (76–80°F), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.023–1.025, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium 380–450 ppm, magnesium 1200–1400 ppm. Agreed on all major parameters. Flow: 'moderate to strong.' Taxonomy note: no reclassification mentioned.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Antillogorgia bipinnata
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Gorgoniidae, Order: Malacalcyonacea), native range (Caribbean, Bahamas, South Florida, 9–27 m depth), adult colony size (~57 cm), and reclassification confirmation: Williams & Chen (2012, Zootaxa) transferred all Caribbean Pseudopterogorgia sea plume species to Antillogorgia. Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Antillogorgia Takes Over as Genus of Caribbean Sea Plume Gorgonians
additional.notes: Used for reclassification context: confirms that all 11 zooxanthellate Caribbean sea plume species previously in Pseudopterogorgia were moved to Antillogorgia (Williams & Chen, 2012, Zootaxa). Not used for husbandry parameters.
